decomposition.title: MOTR PLM Windchill Migration from PTC Cloud to ABB Cloud and Post-GoLive Application Hosting, Maintenance and Monitoring Services
decomposition.industry: Industrial technology / manufacturing IT / PLM application hosting and cloud migration
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: Inferred $5M-$15M over 3-5 years based on scope: end-to-end Windchill PLM migration from PTC Cloud to ABB Cloud, post-GoLive application hosting/maintenance/monitoring services, multiple non-production and production environments, CAD/Office workers, six SAP integrations, global users estimated at 700 Authors and 175 Viewers, 24/7 monitoring, 99.5% monthly availability, service desk/SLA obligations, security/compliance obligations, and optional supplier-hosted infrastructure. No explicit budget is stated.
decomposition.contract_duration: Intended contract period for continuous PLM Hosting services is three (3) or five (5) years, with termination notice of six (6) months during the first three years and three (3) months thereafter; tacit renewal may be incorporated with supplier amendment proposal six months before the next contract anniversary date. [SECTION 9]

requirements.text: Supplier must use ABB-provided terms and conditions as the basis; ABB states that Customer does not accept any T&Cs other than those provided as part of the RFP as discussion basis.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Legal terms are often handled late, but ABB makes acceptance of ABB terms the discussion basis and links unresolved legal issues to down-selection.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: ABB global terms and conditions; referenced ABB GTC IT Procurement 2023-04 and schedules.

requirements.text: If Supplier has an existing agreement, Supplier MUST contact Customer prior to submission of the RFP response to receive confirmation that the agreement may be leveraged.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Applicable only if supplier intends to rely on an existing agreement; failure must occur before submission and can be missed by bid teams.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Existing supplier agreement with ABB, subject to ABB sole discretion.

requirements.text: Supplier is expected to agree applicable legal terms and conditions during the RFP phase so that no material issue remains open; such agreement is crucial for Supplier’s down-selection.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Down-selection dependency is buried in tender phase procedure rather than legal section.

requirements.text: ABB may reject any proposal that does not conform to the instructions and specifications issued in the RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: General reservation of rights creates a rejection risk for any non-conforming proposal.

requirements.text: ABB may not accept proposals after the stated submission deadline.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The actual submission deadline is not present in the provided text, but late proposals may not be accepted.

requirements.text: For optional A3 Hosting outside ABB, the proposed solution must be ABB InfoSec assessed and approved as a prerequisite.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This prerequisite appears only in optional A3 hosting section; suppliers proposing external hosting could be eliminated if not approved.

requirements.text: If Supplier requires any RFP information to be shared with other organizations to answer the RFP, Supplier shall first obtain ABB’s formal written approval.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Subcontractor and third-party involvement can be common in cloud/PLM bids, but disclosure approval is required before sharing RFP information.

requirements.text: Supplier shall impose the same or equivalent confidentiality and non-disclosure obligations on subcontractors or other third parties engaged in relation to the RFP and is responsible and liable for their acts and omissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Supplier remains liable for subcontractors' confidentiality breaches.

requirements.text: Supplier shall not release information to newspapers, trade magazines, journals, or any media about the existence/content of the RFP, proposal acceptance, or possible award without ABB written permission.
Supplier should propose an executable transition plan without tying it to a certain start date, granting flexibility to ABB to organize transition around internal and external dependencies.
Supplier is responsible for providing best-fit services for ABB IS and ABB MOTR and maintaining the service at required quality levels throughout the contract duration.

Supplier must organize services so staff stays highly motivated and works reliably, responsibly, and independently; potential staffing and fluctuation risks requiring ABB attention should be shared without delay.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Staffing fluctuation disclosure obligation can affect service continuity evaluation.

requirements.text: Supplier must propose the target system setup at high level as part of the proposal and provide a detailed setup after winning the bid and before contract execution.
ABB will provision infrastructure and it is not in scope, but the supplier team onboarded under the contract must coordinate with the ABB team provisioning it.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Infrastructure provisioning is excluded from supplier scope, but coordination with ABB infrastructure remains required.

requirements.text: Supplier proposal must cover A1 End to End Cloud Migration from PTC Cloud to ABB Cloud and A2 Post-GoLive Application hosting, maintenance, and monitoring services.
Supplier should clearly indicate if any requested service activities are ABB responsibility or excluded from supplier service.

A1 scope includes assessment and planning: review systems/processes/data/workflows, analyze dependencies affecting integrations/data/systems, assess architecture changes including infrastructure, size Windchill/DB/CAD/Office worker servers, assist ABB Cloud server ordering, analyze infrastructure/security/network requirements, assess version and code management, define transition approach, and provide high-level delivery plan by December 2024.
A1 migration must include one month of Hypercare after Go Live.

A1 migration design must include detailed analysis of system to be migrated/rehosted, fit/gap analysis, version management and migration from existing PTC SVN, release/deployment management process and tool, development standards, architecture diagram showing integrations, LDAP/network/VPN architecture changes, and documented solution approach.

A1 migration development must include development of utilities/custom tools as needed, future system and infrastructure readiness with all integrations in place, acceptance test scripts, and a detailed deployment plan.

A1 migration execution must include end-to-end system and tool setup for migration, migration from PTC Cloud to ABB-provisioned cloud infrastructure, data migration rehearsals, end-to-end acceptance tests with integrations, cutover/deployment execution, migration quality reports, hypercare support, and handover to the pre-existing third-party AH&MS team.
Supplier must set up Windchill systems for Development, Test, Quality/Integration, and Production, including CAD and Office Worker setups for those systems.
Business logic must be ensured; all customizations must work as in the previous environment; migration and future environment must not interfere with any function.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: ABB demands functional equivalence and no interference with existing functions, which can create acceptance risk after migration.

requirements.text: Supplier must hand over to operations, ensure project team equals operations team or operations team escorts the whole project, make knowledge and workforce available, and document/transfer knowledge to operations team.

Supplier must work closely with ABB and integration partners using Agile delivery of about four Product Increments per year, and support planning, code deployment, PI Go-Live, Hypercare, and quick fixes.
Supplier must be able to support larger projects, such as move from PTC Cloud to ABB Cloud, handled in ABB’s classic 0-7 Gate Waterfall model.

AH&MS must receive tickets in Freshdesk or ServiceNow, raise tickets to ABB infrastructure for infrastructure topics, raise tickets to PTC Tech Support for tool issues/bugs, and handle/support deployments of new code.

AH&MS main duties include system monitoring, updates, coordination with different teams, performance assessments, proactive precautions, coordination with PTC Product Support, backup coordination, OS update coordination, end-to-end deployments, application restore from backups, log review/maintenance, maintenance planning, environment restarts, and ticket coordination.

Supplier is accountable to fix any application hosting issue in close collaboration with all teams; if necessary knowledge is not within the team, it must be brought into the team at Supplier’s own expense.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Supplier must absorb knowledge gaps at its own expense, a commercial delivery risk.

requirements.text: Supplier must troubleshoot and fix performance-related incidents/requirements, maximize application reliability and availability, and resolve all performance-related issues independently.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Performance issues must be resolved independently by supplier, not escalated as out-of-scope.

requirements.text: Supplier must propose code updates if code fails due to changes in Supplier service infrastructure, including data center, hardware, monitoring, network, or platform.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Code updates caused by supplier infrastructure changes are included, creating hidden scope expansion.

requirements.text: Supplier must work with third-party applications or integrations included in the service.
Supplier must provide predictive maintenance and reporting before calamities occur; any utility needed for this must be considered part of supplier scope at no extra cost.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Utilities for predictive maintenance are in scope at no extra cost, which may be missed in pricing.

requirements.text: Supplier must make sure any new security modifications suggested by ABB information security team are accepted and implemented.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Security changes are open-ended and must be accepted and implemented.

requirements.text: ABB must have access to all logs and to information on members accessing ABB environments hosted over the cloud; Supplier must provide these logs at regular intervals or on ABB demand.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: ABB requires visibility into environment access logs and application logs on demand or regular intervals.

requirements.text: Supplier must test and install security updates, service packs, application and database components, software components requiring continuous maintenance, new modules, integrations, and third-party software.
Supplier must provide access rights to servers, including to third parties, based on ABB request.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Third-party server access must be granted based on ABB request, impacting access control processes.

requirements.text: Supplier must document the application hosting knowledge base.

Supplier should maintain the following security accreditations: FedRAMP Authorization, RMF for DoD IT, SSAE-16/SOC 2 Type 2 Attestation for Security & Availability Trust Principles, ISO 27001:2022 Certification, and ITAR Compliance.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The accreditations are buried in the AH&MS security subsection and include U.S. federal/defense and export-control items that many suppliers may not hold; wording is 'should maintain', not 'must'.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Includes U.S. federal/DoD and ITAR compliance references.
requirements.cert_required: FedRAMP Authorization; RMF for DoD IT; SSAE-16/SOC 2 Type 2; ISO 27001:2022; ITAR Compliance

requirements.text: Supplier must provide certifications, audit reports, and other standard security information required by ABB at no additional cost to ABB.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Supplier may have to provide audit/security evidence without additional cost.

requirements.text: Supplier shall comply with additional security, audit, and reporting requirements, if any, beyond those specified in the contract.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Open-ended additional security/audit/reporting requirements may be imposed beyond the contract.

requirements.text: ABB or an ABB-hired third party can perform penetration or vulnerability testing of any Cloud application at any point of time.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Penetration/vulnerability testing can occur at any point of time by ABB or third party.

requirements.text: Supplier shall adhere to ABB Disaster Recovery Plan.

Supplier security procedures should span all areas of service including logical and physical security controls, infrastructure security, supplier personnel, and security incident and response process as defined by ABB InfoSec.
Incident management support hours are 7:00 CET to 17:00 CET Monday to Friday; outside these hours, Sev 1 support such as system down is required on-call.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Coverage includes on-call Sev 1 support outside normal hours.

requirements.text: Service request standard operating hours are 7:00 CET to 17:00 CET Monday-Friday; occasional activities may be executed outside standard hours/weekends between 04:00 CET and 18:00 CET Saturday-Sunday by mutual agreement; emergency executions between 23:00 CET and 04:00 CET Monday-Friday must be agreed with Application Manager and Application Owner.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Weekend and emergency support windows may affect staffing and pricing, with on-call/overtime often not chargeable unless agreed.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Service request windows stated in CET.

requirements.text: Supplier shall monitor availability of the Hosted System 24/7.
Production environment availability must be 99.5% measured each month, with 24/5 availability Monday to Friday.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Monthly measured availability commitment is explicit.

requirements.text: Supplier shall transparently report planned and unplanned production downtime and availability percentage in each monthly service review meeting and share metrics afterwards.
Service Request fulfillment target is minimum 90%, calculated as service requests closed within agreed closing time divided by total submitted service requests.
For optional A3, supplier may offer infrastructure services if able to provide a similar setup to current PTC Cloud using Amazon/MS SQL or ABB Cloud using Azure/MS SQL.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: A3 is optional but, if offered, must be similar to current PTC Cloud or ABB Cloud and pass InfoSec approval.

requirements.text: Supplier pricing model must provide a clear understanding of total costs, pursue continuous cost reduction, optimize bought-out goods/services through market investigations, provide transparent pricing with adjustment mechanisms based on defined resource units, and highlight dynamics between cost and service level.

Charges must be complete, transparent, predictable, recognizable, fully cover requested services, and include full compensation for supplier costs including travel and living expenses unless expressly stated otherwise.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Pricing must include all costs unless exceptions are clearly stated; hidden pass-through assumptions may be rejected or unrecoverable.

requirements.text: Charges are independent of Supplier's workload and effort assumptions except for agreed identified cost drivers, which must be clearly highlighted and listed on the Assumption Form if based on assumptions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Supplier workload assumptions cannot drive charges except agreed cost drivers listed in the Assumption Form.

requirements.text: Supplier must specify any monthly recurring charge price items with recurring PxQ charges and separately specify pass-through charges with relevant third-party invoice and backup detail for review.
Supplier must present separate cost for one-time A1 services and monthly/annual/unit charges for continuous A2 and optional A3 services.

Customer preference is fixed price; fixed-price proposal must be broken down by chargeable deliverables, reduced rates apply for significant volumes, and maximum quoted price must not exceed applicable rate times estimated effort.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: ABB preference is fixed price and fixed-price maximum cannot exceed rate times estimated effort.

requirements.text: T&M proposals must identify skill sets, associated rates, and monthly resource utilization over the contract period, with skill sets clearly linked to the Rate Card.

Supplier must deliver a Rate Card specifying day rates for job profiles and seniority levels, covering onshore local and offshore rates; except as specifically agreed otherwise and approved by ABB, Supplier shall not charge for on-call support and overtime.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: On-call support and overtime generally cannot be charged unless specifically agreed and approved.

requirements.text: Implementation charges and rate card rates include all in-country travel and expenses; out-of-country travel is reimbursable only if requested and approved upfront in writing by ABB, without markups and with receipts.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Travel and expenses are largely bundled and unreimbursable without prior written approval.

evaluation_criteria.name: Best fit against requirements and pricing transparency

evaluation_criteria.name: PLM migration, hosting, and operations capability
evaluation_criteria.description: Detailed understanding of best practices for PLM platform migration, upgrade, hosting, implementation, and operations, and how supplier would support ABB.

evaluation_criteria.name: Team skills, expertise, and methodology
evaluation_criteria.description: Detailed understanding of proposed experts' skills and capabilities and methodologies used to guarantee solid project execution and long-term maintenance.

evaluation_criteria.name: Transition and service delivery approach
evaluation_criteria.description: Detailed understanding of processes, tools, skills, and capabilities used to guarantee smooth transition and efficient platform.

evaluation_criteria.name: Effort, cost, and timeline proposal
evaluation_criteria.description: Supplier proposal for effort, cost, and time needed for transition and continuous services.

evaluation_criteria.name: Legal terms acceptance / no material open issues
evaluation_criteria.description: Agreement on applicable legal terms and conditions during RFP phase so no material issues remain open; stated as crucial for down-selection.

evaluation_criteria.name: Commercial model quality
evaluation_criteria.description: Transparent pricing model with complete, predictable charges, defined resource units, adjustment mechanisms, scale up/down principles, and separate pricing for A1, A2, and optional A3.

Hypercare period required after Go Live for migration. [SECTION 39]

ambiguities: Tender planning table is referenced in [SECTION 13], but no actual dates or submission deadline are included in the provided document text.

Tables and figures are referenced throughout the document, but the actual table/figure contents are not included, limiting extraction of exact environment counts, user distribution by country, storage consumption, SLA values, severity matrices, KPI metric details, and governance cadence.

Security accreditations in [SECTION 43] are introduced with 'Supplier should maintain' rather than 'must' or 'shall'; they are treated as critical must-have/hidden-risk requirements but not as definitive eliminatory requirements.

The document states ABB expects legal terms to be agreed during the RFP phase with no material issues and that this is crucial for down-selection, but does not define exactly which legal deviations are fatal.
A3 hosting outside ABB is optional; however, if proposed, ABB InfoSec assessment and approval is stated as a prerequisite. This is eliminatory only for suppliers proposing optional A3 hosting.

The document requests an 'overall understanding of possible cost of services' but provides no budget or incumbent spend; deal size is therefore inferred from scope, users, integrations, migration, support model, monitoring, and contract duration.
hard_disqualifiers: LEGAL TERMS DISQUALIFIER: ABB states 'Customer does not accept any T&Cs other than the ones provided as part of this RFP as discussion basis.' [SECTION 10]

EXISTING AGREEMENT PRE-SUBMISSION DISQUALIFIER: 'In case of an existing agreement, Supplier MUST contact Customer prior to submission of the RFP response in order to receive confirmation from Customer, that respective agreement may be leveraged for the purposes.' [SECTION 10]

DOWN-SELECTION LEGAL DISQUALIFIER: ABB expects legal terms to be agreed during the RFP phase 'to the extent that no material issue remain open' and states 'Such agreement will be considered crucial for Supplier’s down-selection.' [SECTION 13]
NON-CONFORMING PROPOSAL REJECTION RISK: ABB may 'Reject any proposal that does not conform to instructions and specifications that are issued herein.' [SECTION 11]
LATE SUBMISSION REJECTION RISK: ABB states it may 'Not accept proposals after the stated submission deadline.' [SECTION 11]
OPTIONAL A3 HOSTING DISQUALIFIER: For supplier-hosted infrastructure outside ABB, 'Prerequisite is, that such a Solution is ABB InfoSec assessed and approved.' [SECTION 52]
compliance_red_flags: ABB does not accept supplier terms and conditions other than ABB-provided T&Cs as the discussion basis.
If an existing agreement is proposed for leverage, supplier MUST contact ABB before RFP response submission for confirmation that it may be used.
Agreement on applicable legal terms and conditions during the RFP phase, with no material issue remaining open, is stated as crucial for down-selection.
Any sharing of RFP confidential information with subcontractors or other organizations requires ABB formal written approval first.
Optional supplier-hosted infrastructure outside ABB Cloud must be ABB InfoSec assessed and approved.

Supplier is expected to maintain significant security accreditations including FedRAMP Authorization, RMF for DoD IT, SOC 2 Type 2, ISO 27001:2022, and ITAR compliance, although the wording uses 'should'.
ABB or ABB-appointed third party may perform penetration or vulnerability testing of any Cloud application at any time.
Supplier must accept and implement new security modifications suggested by ABB information security team.
Supplier shall adhere to ABB Disaster Recovery Plan and additional security, audit, and reporting requirements.

ABB may refuse any configuration, customization, approach, methodology, or integration considered a risk for performance, standards, maintainability, operations, security, or information/data security.

similar_deals.outcome: Proposal precedent; outcome not provided in the supplied knowledge base
similar_deals.why_relevant: Closest proposal precedent for PLM migration, manufacturing context, global users, multiple environments, Azure/cloud architecture, DR/high availability, performance/load testing, and pricing within the inferred ABB range.
similar_deals.key_lesson: PLM migration should be positioned around migration acceleration, multi-environment readiness, DR/performance validation, security, and minimized downtime rather than a simple technical move.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 38% with medium-low confidence. Positive evidence: the knowledge base contains a strong analogous PLM migration pricing and solution precedent at $8.32M for Lam Research, a PLM-adjacent hosting/managed services precedent for Bridgestone, manufacturing/cloud hosting methodology from Kohler, and deal-corpus wins in Germany/manufacturing or Germany/managed transformation where domain credibility and rapid mobilisation were decisive. These support a credible bid if positioned around industrial PLM migration, SAP integration assurance, 24/7 managed operations, and fast mobilisation. Negative evidence materially limits the probability: no exact prior ABB Windchill migration from PTC Cloud to ABB Cloud was found; the only ABB-specific prior proposal is much smaller and not PLM; public procurement data is unavailable, so competitor/incumbent strength and actual award prices cannot be validated; and ABB has several eliminatory legal/procedural gates that can disqualify the bid before technical scoring. The probability is therefore below 50% unless legal acceptance, existing-agreement confirmation, Windchill-specific proof points, InfoSec handling for optional hosting, and a compliant transition plan are fixed before submission. If ABB T&Cs or pre-submission agreement confirmation cannot be satisfied, practical win probability should be treated as near zero regardless of technical capability.
capability_gaps: No exact prior proposal or deal-corpus record was found for an ABB Windchill migration specifically from PTC Cloud to ABB Cloud; this is directly relevant to the mandatory A1 scope requiring end-to-end cloud migration from PTC Cloud to ABB Cloud.

Windchill-specific production hosting proof is only indirectly evidenced through Lam PLM migration and Bridgestone 3DExperience hosting precedents; the RFP requires A2 post-GoLive Windchill application hosting, maintenance, monitoring, SLA support, and 99.5% monthly availability.
No evidence was provided that optional supplier-hosted infrastructure has already been ABB InfoSec assessed and approved; this is a prerequisite for optional A3 hosting outside ABB.

Legal acceptance of ABB-provided terms is not evidenced; ABB states its own T&Cs must be used as the discussion basis and legal terms must be substantially agreed during the RFP phase for down-selection.
If an existing agreement with ABB is intended to be used, there is no evidence that ABB has confirmed it may be leveraged before proposal submission; this is explicitly mandatory in Section 10.

SAP integration capability is evidenced by broader SAP transformation experience, but not specifically by Windchill-to-SAP interface migration and operational monitoring for the six SAP integrations required in this RFP.

Prior ABB experience found in the knowledge base is limited to ABB MOTR monitoring/vulnerability improvement content at approximately $182K, not a comparable multi-year PLM migration and managed hosting engagement.
recommended_win_themes: Low-risk industrial PLM migration: position the solution as a controlled Windchill modernization from PTC Cloud to ABB Cloud using migration rehearsals, validation gates, rollback planning, DR/performance testing, and hypercare, drawing on the Lam PLM migration precedent.

Run-ready global Windchill managed service: emphasize 24/7 monitoring, ITIL operations, SLA dashboards, incident/problem/change management, vulnerability management, monthly service reviews, and 99.5% availability governance, drawing on Bridgestone PLM-adjacent hosting and Kohler managed-hosting methods.

SAP-integrated engineering continuity: make the six SAP integrations a named workstream with interface regression testing, cutover sequencing, monitoring, error handling, and accountable integration ownership, supported by SAP/global manufacturing lessons from DEAL-003.

Fast mobilisation with industrial domain credentials: use the deal-corpus pattern from DEAL-089, DEAL-025, and DEAL-069 where domain credibility and rapid team readiness were decisive; commit to a named mobilisation team and transition calendar.

ABB-compliant, security-first operating model: lead with full adherence to ABB RFP instructions, ABB T&Cs, confidentiality flow-down to subcontractors, security controls, auditability, vulnerability management, data protection, and InfoSec approval handling for any optional hosting outside ABB.
risk_factors: No public procurement award data is available, so competitor pricing and incumbent advantage cannot be validated against ground-truth awards.

No exact historical win for ABB Windchill migration from PTC Cloud to ABB Cloud was found; closest evidence is analogous PLM migration, PLM hosting, manufacturing IT transformation, and ABB monitoring work.

ABB’s legal and submission requirements are eliminatory; unresolved T&C exceptions, late submission, non-conforming response, or failure to obtain existing-agreement confirmation could disqualify the bid regardless of technical quality.
Optional supplier-hosted infrastructure carries a specific ABB InfoSec approval prerequisite and should not be positioned as primary unless approval is achievable.
The strongest ABB-specific prior proposal evidence is much smaller and operationally narrower than this RFP, creating limited direct relationship/proof advantage.
The inferred $5M-$15M size is plausible but unconfirmed; underpricing could expose delivery risk, while pricing above the market cannot be checked due to lack of procurement data.

Windchill/CAD/Office worker support, SAP integrations, global users, multi-environment operations, 24/7 monitoring, and security obligations create a broad scope that may be evaluated as a single high-risk transition unless the proposal decomposes ownership and acceptance gates clearly.
must_fix_before_bidding: Obtain legal/commercial approval to use ABB-provided terms and conditions as the proposal basis, with no material unresolved exceptions before down-selection.
If relying on any existing ABB agreement, contact ABB before submission and obtain written confirmation that the agreement may be leveraged.

Decide whether to propose only ABB-provisioned infrastructure or optional supplier-hosted A3 infrastructure; if A3 is included, secure or explicitly plan for ABB InfoSec assessment and approval as a bid gate.

Produce a Windchill-specific transition plan covering PTC Cloud extraction, ABB Cloud target setup, environment build, migration rehearsals, validation, cutover, rollback, hypercare, and steady-state operations without dependency on a fixed start date.
Prepare named Windchill, CAD/Office worker support, SAP integration, cloud operations, security, and service management leads to prove rapid mobilisation and reduce perceived delivery risk.
Build a requirement-by-requirement compliance matrix to avoid ABB’s non-conforming proposal rejection risk.
Create a 3-year and 5-year commercial model in the $8M-$12M core range, with optional discovery/design and supplier-hosted infrastructure priced separately.

draft.executive_summary:
ABB has framed IT as an enterprise transformation enabler, not back-office support, while its 2026 digital agenda embeds cloud, edge, virtualization, analytics, and AI into the ABB portfolio and scales AI adoption across 250+ projects and solutions. The MOTR Windchill move from PTC Cloud to ABB Cloud should therefore be treated as a controlled step into ABB’s Future Hosting Model, not as a basic rehost.

We propose Balanced ABB-Cloud Migration and Managed Windchill Operations: a 36-month, $8.650M service covering A1 end-to-end migration and A2 post-GoLive hosting, maintenance, and monitoring on ABB-provided infrastructure. Our commitment is redline-free, ABB-T&Cs-first execution with a named PLM migration core team, two migration rehearsals, SAP-interface regression certification for all six SAP integrations, rollback, DR and performance gates, 24/7 hypercare, and a run-ready ITIL operating model targeting 99.5% availability governance.

This approach directly addresses ABB’s unstated needs: migration risk controls for CAD/data migration and UAT, landing-zone fit for modular standardized infrastructure, and AI-enabled observability with less human intervention. It gives ABB evidence-led cutover confidence, transparent pricing, FinOps visibility, security auditability, and global service continuity across Switzerland, Germany, Poland, Italy, the USA, India, and Australia. We will protect engineering productivity during migration and leave ABB with a secure, observable, and continuously improving Windchill service.
win_themes: Low-risk industrial PLM migration using rehearsals, validation gates, rollback planning, DR/performance testing, and hypercare.
Run-ready global Windchill managed service with 24/7 monitoring, ITIL operations, SLA dashboards, vulnerability management, and 99.5% availability governance.
SAP-integrated engineering continuity with a dedicated workstream for all six SAP integrations, regression certification, cutover sequencing, monitoring, and accountable ownership.
Fast mobilisation with industrial domain credentials through a named PLM migration core team and transition calendar.
ABB-compliant, security-first operating model with ABB T&Cs alignment, confidentiality flow-down, auditability, vulnerability management, data protection, and InfoSec evidence gating.

sections.section_title: Best fit against requirements and pricing transparency
sections.content:
ABB’s requirement is not a simple Windchill rehost. It is a resilience, cost, architecture, security, and operating-model decision for a global manufacturer whose engineering users, CAD data, SAP interfaces, and product records must continue to perform while hosting moves from PTC Cloud into ABB Cloud. Our recommended Balanced ABB-Cloud Migration and Managed Windchill Operations solution covers A1 migration and A2 post-GoLive hosting, maintenance, and monitoring for 36 months at a transparent price of $8,650,000.

The model is open-book by design: people $4.147M, monitoring/tooling/cloud metering $0.331M, migration $1.150M, governance $0.550M, 12% contingency $0.741M, and 20% margin $1.730M. We separate A1 migration, A2 run services, and optional A3 supplier-hosting decision support, with any A3 option gated by an ABB InfoSec evidence pack.

Swift implementation is enabled through a named mobilisation team, a contractually gated migration factory, two migration rehearsals, SAP-interface regression certification for all six SAP integrations, rollback, DR and performance gates, and 24/7 hypercare-to-run handoff. This gives ABB a low-risk industrial PLM migration with pricing that can be audited, governed, and adjusted as demand changes.

sections.section_title: PLM migration, hosting, and operations capability
sections.content:
Our PLM migration capability is built around a Windchill Migration Factory rather than a one-time cutover event. We begin with discovery of Windchill versions, CAD worker dependencies, vaults, file stores, database objects, customizations, user groups, batch jobs, certificates, middleware, and the six SAP integrations. We then baseline performance, backup, RTO/RPO, security controls, data volumes, and regional usage across Switzerland, Germany, Poland, Italy, the USA, India, and Australia.

Target build will use ABB-provided infrastructure and ABB Cloud landing-zone standards. We configure environments, network paths, identity integration, encryption, backup, monitoring, logging, and DR to fit ABB’s Future Hosting Model. Data migration is executed through extract, transfer, load, reconciliation, and business validation cycles, with two full rehearsals before production cutover. Each rehearsal produces defect logs, duration evidence, rollback proof, and go/no-go recommendations.

Operations are designed from day one. Windchill administrators, DBA/SRE resources, integration specialists, and service managers will operate the platform under ITIL incident, problem, change, release, configuration, vulnerability, and capacity processes. AI-enabled observability will cover 12 critical endpoints, 8 locations, 3,000 metrics, 6,000 GB/month logs, and 30 dashboards. Availability governance targets 99.5%, backed by SLA dashboards, monthly service reviews, and continuous improvement. The result is a controlled modernization that protects engineering continuity while moving ABB from PTC Cloud to ABB Cloud.

ABB will receive a named mobilisation team, not an anonymous capacity pool. The core team comprises a PLM migration architect, Windchill technical lead, CAD/data migration lead, SAP integration lead, ABB Cloud infrastructure lead, DBA/SRE lead, security and vulnerability lead, ITIL service manager, test manager, and cutover manager. Delivery is sized at 15 average FTE across a global model, with offshore-led execution and senior architecture, integration, and service-management leadership available for ABB workshops and decision forums.

Our method follows the industrial PLM pattern used in Lam Research-style cloud migration work: mobilise and assess, design target architecture, build and migrate, test and validate, cut over, hypercare, and operate. Each phase has entry criteria, exit criteria, accountable owners, and client-owned artefacts. The team will maintain a RAID log, decision log, dependency map, integrated cutover plan, security evidence tracker, and service-readiness checklist.

For long-term maintenance, the same knowledge base that executes migration transfers into run support through shadow, reverse-shadow, and handoff gates. Skills include Windchill administration, database operations, cloud operations, integration monitoring, SAP interface triage, scripting, release support, performance tuning, backup recovery, vulnerability remediation, and ITIL governance. This fast mobilisation model gives ABB industrial domain credibility, continuity of knowledge, and a practical team structure ready to support global engineering users from transition through steady state.

Transition will be run as an evidence-led Control Tower with daily execution discipline and weekly ABB decision cadence. In weeks 1–4 we mobilise governance, confirm RFP scope, complete application and infrastructure discovery, validate ABB Cloud prerequisites, and publish the integrated plan. In weeks 5–10 we build non-production environments, configure monitoring, define backup and DR, map data migration, and certify the SAP integration test strategy. In weeks 11–18 we execute migration rehearsal 1, remediate defects, execute rehearsal 2, and complete performance, security, DR, and rollback evidence.

Cutover will be dependency-aware. CAD publishing, vault synchronization, database freeze, SAP interface sequencing, user communication, access validation, and batch scheduling will be controlled through a minute-by-minute runbook. No production go-live will proceed without ABB sign-off against migration reconciliation, six-interface regression certification, DR test completion, performance baseline, vulnerability position, and support readiness.

Post go-live, hypercare operates 24/7 with war-room governance, priority triage, daily service reporting, and clear defect ownership. After stability criteria are met, support transitions to steady-state ITIL operations with SLA dashboards, monthly service reviews, QBRs, problem trend analysis, capacity and FinOps reviews, and an improvement backlog. This approach protects manufacturing and engineering continuity while creating a run-ready global Windchill service.

The proposed effort is a balanced 36-month programme covering A1 migration and A2 managed operations. We assume 15 average FTE across PLM architecture, Windchill administration, CAD/data migration, SAP integration, DBA/SRE, cloud operations, security, testing, service management, and governance. The first six months focus on mobilisation, discovery, target build, migration rehearsals, cutover, and hypercare. Months 7–36 provide steady-state hosting, maintenance, monitoring, release support, vulnerability management, reporting, and continuous improvement.

Our price is $8,650,000. The A1 migration component is $1.150M for discovery, target setup, data migration validation, two rehearsals, cutover, rollback planning, DR/performance gates, and hypercare. A2 run services are principally covered by people cost of $4.147M and monitoring/tooling/cloud metering of $0.331M. Governance is $0.550M, contingency is $0.741M, and margin is $1.730M. Charges are predictable and tied to defined service units: FTE capacity, monitored endpoints, locations, metrics, log volume, dashboards, migration milestones, governance forums, and service reports.

Timeline acceleration is supported by immediate mobilisation, use of reusable PLM migration artefacts, and early ABB Cloud prerequisite tracking. Where ABB increases scope, environments, integrations, locations, or log volumes, we will use agreed rate cards and change control. This gives ABB cost certainty with the flexibility needed for PLM harmonization.

sections.section_title: Legal terms acceptance / no material open issues
sections.content:
Legal alignment is a down-selection issue, and our proposal is structured to remove avoidable friction. We will submit on an ABB-T&Cs-first basis, with no material open legal issues intended for negotiation after selection. Confidentiality, data protection, security, auditability, service governance, subcontractor flow-down, and compliance obligations will be accepted and operationalised through the project governance model.

For security-first execution, we will maintain an ABB InfoSec evidence pack covering architecture, access controls, encryption, monitoring, logging, vulnerability management, backup, DR, privileged access, data handling, and operational procedures. Any optional A3 supplier-hosting decision outside ABB-provided infrastructure will not proceed without ABB InfoSec review, evidence submission, risk acceptance, and written approval. Subcontractors, if any, will be disclosed, contractually bound to ABB confidentiality and security requirements, and governed through the same service controls.

Change, incident, problem, release, and vulnerability processes will preserve audit trails and approval evidence. Commercial assumptions, responsibilities, and exclusions will be documented in the contract schedules so delivery teams execute what ABB approves. This redline-free, ABB-compliant operating stance reinforces the security-first win theme and helps ABB move from sourcing decision to mobilisation without losing calendar time to unresolved terms.

Our commercial model separates what ABB buys, how it scales, and how it is governed. A1 migration is milestone-based against discovery completion, target environment readiness, rehearsal 1, rehearsal 2, SAP-interface regression certification, cutover readiness, production cutover, and hypercare exit. A2 managed services are capacity and service-unit based, covering named roles, 24/7 monitoring, incident and problem management, change and release support, vulnerability management, reporting, and governance. Optional A3 will be priced separately only if ABB requests a supplier-hosted alternative.

Adjustment mechanisms are explicit. FTE capacity scales by agreed role rate cards and notice periods. Observability scales by critical endpoints, locations, metrics, monthly log volume, dashboards, and retention requirements. Additional environments, integrations, CAD workers, data migration cycles, releases, or regulatory evidence packs are handled through change control with impact on cost, schedule, and risk stated before approval. Savings opportunities are reviewed through FinOps and capacity forums.

The model is designed for predictable charges, no hidden run costs, and ABB-owned transparency. Monthly reports will show service consumption, SLA performance, open risks, changes, incidents, vulnerabilities, backlog, and forecasted demand. Quarterly business reviews will confirm whether the commercial baseline remains aligned to ABB’s PLM harmonization and cloud governance priorities. This gives ABB a competitive price with the control mechanisms needed for global industrial operations.

critical_issues: Infosys financial claims are contradicted by the provided SEC EDGAR data: agent claims FY26 operating margin of about 20.3%, while the raw financial data lists Infosys operating margin as 37.22%. Any Infosys margin-floor pricing derived from 20.3% should be removed or recalculated using the provided data.

Accenture financial calculations are unsupported: the raw SEC EDGAR section says Accenture financial data is unavailable, but the agent claims Q3 FY2026 operating margin, FY2025 revenue of about $70B, 799,000 employees, and revenue-per-employee calculations.

TCS and Capgemini financial-margin claims are unsupported by the provided financial data. TCS and Capgemini are not present in the raw SEC EDGAR data, so their FY2026/FY2025 margin-based price floors cannot be verified.

Most competitor capability claims rely on unspecified web search/job-posting evidence that is not included in the raw sources. Claims about TCS ABB MoU, Windchill hiring, Infosys PTC alliance credentials, Capgemini Piterion acquisition, Accenture Windchill postings, NATO announcement, and similar items are unverified.

The claim of a 'named Lam-proven Windchill migration team' is inaccurate against the knowledge base. The Lam precedent is a PLM migration / Teamcenter-related analogue, not a verified Windchill migration precedent.
The win-probability rationale cites 'deal-corpus wins in Germany/manufacturing or Germany/managed transformation' but no such deal-corpus records are present in the provided raw data.

ABB legal/procedural gate claims are not supported by the provided raw sources, including claims about ABB T&Cs, legal terms being substantially agreed during the RFP phase, Section 10 existing-agreement confirmation, and down-selection consequences.
The optional A3 hosting / ABB InfoSec approval prerequisite is not evidenced in the provided raw data. It may be true if in the RFP, but the RFP text was not supplied here.

The phrase 'public procurement source states no public procurement data available' overstates the raw data. The procurement data contains unrelated UK/US awards and no relevant ABB Windchill awards; it does not explicitly state that public procurement data is unavailable.

Recommended price is directionally plausible because it is close to the verified Lam $8.32M PLM migration benchmark and within the $5M-$15M range, but confidence should be lowered because no detailed cost model, cloud API values, ABB budget, or relevant public award prices are provided.
removed_claims: TCS has a recent ABB strategic MoU in the exact account context.
TCS has active Windchill hiring in India, Germany manufacturing/SAP hiring signals, public PLM-cloud/Windchill capability, and recent cloud/managed-services momentum.
TCS FY2026 operating margin is 25.0% and supports a margin-floor price of 1.33C.
Infosys FY26 operating margin is about 20.3%, with FY27 margin guidance of 20%-22%, as a basis for price-floor calculations.

Infosys has public PTC/Windchill alliance credentials, Advanced Partner status for Windchill, a dedicated Windchill-on-AWS migration paper, a new Zurich office, and an active U.S. Senior Windchill System Administrator posting.
Capgemini FY2025 operating margin is 13.3% and supports a margin-floor price of 1.15C.
Capgemini has active Windchill roles in India, USA and Europe, and acquired/announced acquisition of Piterion as a Stuttgart PLM/MOM specialist with Italy and India subsidiaries.
Accenture Q3 FY2026 operating margin is 17.0%, FY2025 revenue is about $70B, employee count is approximately 799,000, and revenue per employee is $87,609-$93,717.

Accenture has multiple direct PTC Windchill/PLM job postings in the USA, Italy, Switzerland and Germany/Europe, recent secure multi-cloud and managed-services wins, NATO evidence, and strong Industry X momentum.
All competitor likelihood ratings of 'high' with 'high confidence' should be removed or downgraded because the supporting web/job-posting evidence is not present in the raw data.
The win-probability rationale claim about deal-corpus wins in Germany/manufacturing or Germany/managed transformation where domain credibility and rapid mobilisation were decisive.
The phrase 'named Lam-proven Windchill migration team' and any implication that Lam is a Windchill-specific precedent.
Any claim that ABB legal terms, ABB T&Cs, or Section 10 existing-agreement confirmation are mandatory gates unless the actual RFP text is supplied.
Any claim that optional supplier-hosted infrastructure has an ABB InfoSec pre-approval prerequisite unless the actual RFP text is supplied.

The claim that the public procurement source states no public procurement data is available; replace with: 'provided procurement records show no relevant ABB Windchill/PTC Cloud-to-ABB Cloud award data.'
warnings: Core knowledge-base anchors are verified: Lam $8.32M PLM migration benchmark, Kohler hosting/cloud transformation methodology and prices, Bridgestone 3DExperience managed-services benchmark, and ABB MOTR $182,336 non-PLM precedent.
The recommended $8.65M price is plausible but not directly verified; it is an extrapolation from the Lam benchmark rather than a costed bottom-up estimate.
No relevant public procurement award data for ABB Windchill/PTC Cloud-to-ABB Cloud migration is present in the supplied procurement records.
The competitor analysis should be treated as low-confidence unless the underlying web-search/job-posting sources are provided for verification.
The provided 'CLOUD PRICING DATA' only says Azure/AWS APIs were validated, but no actual cloud pricing values are supplied, so it cannot materially validate the proposed price.

RFP-specific scope details such as six SAP integrations, 99.5% monthly availability, 24/7 monitoring, and approximately 700 Authors / 175 Viewers are supported only insofar as they appear inside the provided knowledge-base narrative, not from an attached primary RFP document.

Adjusted win probability lowered from 0.38 to 0.30 because several positive and negative drivers are unsupported, although the Lam/Kohler/Bridgestone analogues do provide some credible basis for bidding.
